# Data and Models

## Data

Mutation counts were obtained from the TCGA PanCancer Atlas [@weinstein2013tcga;
@hoadley2018cell] via the cBioPortal API [@cbioportal] using the `cbioportalR` R
package. For each of $G = 10$ genes (TP53, KRAS, PIK3CA, BRAF, PTEN, APC,
ARID1A, EGFR, IDH1, CDKN2A) and $C = 20$ cancer types with at least 150
sequenced tumors, we computed $y_{gc}$, the number of tumors with a mutation in gene $g$ in cancer type $c$, and $n_{gc}$, the
total number of sequenced tumors for that cancer type to give us $y_{gc}/n_{gc}$ is the observed mutation prevalence. This gives
$N = G \times C = 200$ observations. Observed rates range from 0.004 (TP53 in
thyroid) to 0.83 (TP53 in lung squamous).

## Model 1: Independent Beta-Binomial (baseline)

A conjugate baseline assumes every (gene, cancer) pair is independent:
\begin{align}
y_{gc} \mid \theta_{gc} &\sim \text{Binomial}(n_{gc}, \theta_{gc}) \\
\theta_{gc} &\sim \text{Beta}(1, 1).
\end{align}
This returns a Beta$(1 + y_{gc},\, 1 + n_{gc} - y_{gc})$ posterior per cell
and cannot share information across cancer types.

## Model 2: Hierarchical Beta-Binomial (partial pooling)

We pool within each gene by placing a gene-level prior on the rates:
\begin{align}
y_{gc} \mid \theta_{gc} &\sim \text{Binomial}(n_{gc}, \theta_{gc}) \\
\theta_{gc} \mid \mu_g, s_g &\sim \text{Beta}(\mu_g s_g,\, (1 - \mu_g) s_g) \\
\mu_g &\sim \text{Beta}(1, 1),\quad s_g \sim \text{Exponential}(0.1).
\end{align}
The $(\mu_g, s_g)$ parameterization is interpretable: $\mu_g$ is gene $g$'s
typical rate across cancer types and $s_g$ is its concentration. Large $s_g$
means the gene behaves consistently across tissues, small $s_g$ means
heterogeneity. The $\text{Exp}(0.1)$ prior on $s_g$ has mean 10 and is weakly
informative, placing substantial mass over a wide range of concentration values. This allows both weak ($s \approx 2$) and strong pooling
($s \gg 50$), while still regularizing extreme values.

Identifiability note: with 20 observations per gene, per-gene hyperparameters
are well-identified. We do not add a further hyper-prior pooling across genes,
as 10 genes provides limited information for a second hierarchical level
[@bda3, ch. 5].
